blocks.body: Verb Patterns help you see when a verb is followed by a gerund, a to-infinitive, or a bare infinitive, and how the meaning changes with the pattern.

{"level":"A2-B2","intro":"In this branch you will gradually see how -ing, to + verb, and the base verb work after different verbs, and when the change of form changes the message itself.","rules":[{"label":"Pattern after the verb","color":"emerald","text":"After some verbs we naturally use a gerund, after others a to-infinitive, and after modal verbs or structures like let / make we use the bare infinitive.","example":"enjoy reading / decide to go / can swim"},{"label":"Meaning changes too","color":"blue","text":"With some pairs, the same verb plus a different pattern creates a different meaning: stop smoking and stop to smoke are not the same idea.","example":"remember locking / remember to lock"},{"label":"Learn the model, not only the word","color":"amber","text":"It is best to remember not only the verb but the full pattern together with an example and a common mistake.","example":"suggest going, not suggest to go"}]}

{"title":"1. What This Branch Covers","intro":"The first version of Verb Patterns stays flat and clear: one category with eight direct lessons in a natural sequence.","rows":[{"en":"Gerund","ua":"the -ing form used as an action, activity, or process","note":"Swimming is fun. / I enjoy reading."},{"en":"To-Infinitive","ua":"to + base verb for intention, plan, purpose, and many verb patterns","note":"want to go / decide to stay"},{"en":"Bare Infinitive","ua":"the base form without to after modals, let, make, and help","note":"can drive / let me go"},{"en":"Gerund vs Infinitive","ua":"the contrast between the two patterns after different or the same verbs","note":"like reading / like to read"},{"en":"Verbs + Gerund","ua":"enjoy, avoid, suggest, finish, mind, keep, consider","note":"suggest going / keep working"},{"en":"Verbs + Infinitive","ua":"want, hope, decide, plan, promise, refuse, need","note":"plan to leave / refuse to answer"},{"en":"Stop / Remember / Forget / Try / Regret","ua":"verbs where changing the pattern often changes the meaning","note":"stop doing / stop to do"},{"en":"Be used to / Get used to / Used to","ua":"being accustomed, becoming accustomed, and past habits","note":"be used to working / used to live"}],"warning":"⚠ After prepositions we normally need a gerund, and after modal verbs we need a bare infinitive. Do not swap these patterns."}

{"title":"2. How to Study This Branch","items":["First fix the three core forms: gerund, to-infinitive, and bare infinitive.","Then move to the common verb lists that usually take one pattern: verbs + gerund and verbs + infinitive.","Pay special attention to verbs where the pattern changes the meaning, especially stop, remember, forget, try, regret.","At the end, compare be used to, get used to, and used to so you do not mix up being accustomed, becoming accustomed, and past habits.","In every lesson, learn not only the rule but also a ready-made example and a common mistake beside the correct form."]}
